text: The surgeon positions the Maryland bipolar forceps in the left robotic arm, monopolar scissors in the right arm, and tenaculum forceps in the third arm, preparing for precise manipulation during the prostatectomy.
The sigmoid colon is visible below, with the outline of the bladder discernible ahead in the surgical field.
The surgeon makes an incision lateral to the left medial umbilical ligament, initiating the surgical approach for the prostatectomy.
The surgeon makes an incision lateral to the right medial umbilical ligament, mirroring the previous incision on the left side.
After completing the incisions, the surgeon transects the medial umbilical ligaments and the urachus using robotic instruments, with the patient positioned in a steep Trendelenburg position.
The surgeon develops the space of Retzius using a combination of blunt dissection and cautery, manipulating the tissue layers to create a clear surgical field.
The surgeon continues the dissection towards the pubic bone, with the bladder and prostate visible in the surgical field.
With the space of Retzius developed, the surgeon manipulates the Foley catheter to visually identify the bladder neck, which is positioned adjacent to the prostate.
The surgeon opens the bladder transversely, making an incision approximately three centimeters above the bladder neck, revealing the internal bladder structures.
The surgeon uses monopolar cautery to continue dissection until the Foley catheter balloon becomes visible within the surgical field.
The surgeon uses tenaculum forceps to elevate the anterior lip of the bladder wall, providing retraction and improved visibility of the surgical field.
The surgeon continues to open the bladder wall, extending the incision until reaching the lateral limits of the prostate, with the bladder and prostate visible in the surgical field.
Bladder stones are visually identified and removed using a specimen retrieval bag inserted through the eleven millimeter assistant port.
The surgeon removes five bladder stones using a specimen retrieval bag through the assistant port, with the stones clearly visible as they are extracted from the bladder.
The surgeon uses a tenaculum to identify the posterior bladder neck, providing a clear view of the anatomical structure during the prostatectomy procedure.
The surgeon begins the dissection by entering the plane between the prostate adenoma and the prostatic capsule, carefully separating these structures.
The surgeon uses a combination of bipolar and monopolar energy to cauterize tissue and control bleeding within the surgical field.
The surgeon uses blunt dissection and cautery to extend the circumferential plane of dissection around the prostate adenoma, carefully separating it from the surrounding prostatic capsule.
The surgeon uses a tenaculum to aid in retracting tissue, providing better exposure of the surgical field during the prostatectomy.
The prostatic adenoma is visibly separating from the prostatic capsule, revealing a clear plane between the two structures.
The surgeon moves the dissection anteriorly after reaching the posterior and lateral limits, continuing to separate the prostate adenoma from the prostatic capsule.
The surgeon places a zero bicoral state suture on a CT needle into the anterior bladder wall, securing it as a holding stitch.
The surgeon uses a fascial closure device to bring the suture through the anterior abdominal wall, securing the tissue in place.
The surgeon continues anterior dissection around the prostate, carefully separating the prostate tissue from surrounding structures.
The prostatic adenoma is visibly almost detached from the surrounding capsule, indicating near completion of the separation process.
The surgeon places the fully freed adenoma into a specimen retrieval bag for removal from the surgical field.
The surgical specimen is removed in two pieces, indicating the completion of the prostatectomy procedure.
The surgeon uses microsutures to control the pedicles located at the five and seven o'clock positions around the prostate, ensuring precise management of these anatomical structures.
The surgeon uses a 2-0 microsuture to approximate the urethra to the trigone of the bladder, ensuring precise alignment of these structures.
The surgeon uses a surgical slip knot to secure the adenoma, which is nearly free, facilitating its placement into a specimen retrieval bag for removal.
The surgeon places a total of five interrupted 2-0 microsutures, securing the anatomical structures involved in the prostatectomy procedure.
The surgeon carefully removes the urethral stents from the surgical field, completing this step of the prostatectomy procedure.
The surgeon inserts a 22 French three-way Foley catheter into the urethra, ensuring proper placement for urinary drainage.
The surgeon closes the bladder using two layers of running 2-0 Vicryl sutures, ensuring secure approximation of the bladder tissue.
The surgeon demonstrates the placement of the first layer of sutures, likely on the bladder, using running two ovicral sutures as previously mentioned.
The surgeon fills the bladder with 200 cc of normal saline, visibly distending the bladder as part of the procedure.
The surgical field is inspected, showing no visible evidence of leakage from the bladder closure.
The surgeon completes the closure of the bladder by finishing the second layer with running sutures, ensuring the bladder is securely sealed.
The surgeon places a closed suction device near the surgical site, ensuring proper drainage post-prostatectomy.
Under laparoscopic visualization, the surgeon removes all trocars from the abdominal cavity, ensuring clear observation of the surgical field during extraction.
The assistant port is closed, ensuring the surgical site is secure following the procedure.
The surgeon removes the prostatectomy specimen and proceeds to close the skin and fascia layers, completing the surgical procedure.
